meta.name: UCLA Loneliness Scale
meta.description: The UCLA Loneliness Scale is a 20-item measure assessing subjective feelings of loneliness and social isolation.
meta.author: Russell D (1996)
meta.reference: Russell D. UCLA Loneliness Scale (Version 3): Reliability, validity, and factor structure. J Pers Assess. 1996;66(1):20-40.
meta.instruction: Indicate how often each of the statements below is descriptive of you.

questions.text: I feel in tune with the people around me.
I lack companionship.
There is no one I can turn to.
I do not feel alone.
I feel part of a group of friends.
I have a lot in common with the people around me.
I am no longer close to anyone.
My interests and ideas are not shared by those around me.
I am an outgoing person.
There are people I feel close to.
I feel left out.
My social relationships are superficial.
No one really knows me well.
I feel isolated from others.
I can find companionship when I want it.
There are people who really understand me.
I am unhappy being so withdrawn.
People are around me but not with me.
There are people I can talk to.
There are people I can turn to.

ranges.label: Low Loneliness
ranges.description: Your loneliness score is low, indicating that you generally feel socially connected and satisfied with your relationships. You likely have meaningful bonds with others and do not frequently experience isolation or disconnection in daily life.
suggestions: Continue nurturing the relationships and social habits that contribute to your sense of connection
Consider reaching out to others who may be experiencing loneliness, as your support can make a meaningful difference
Stay engaged in community activities, clubs, or groups that align with your interests and values

ranges.label: Moderate Loneliness
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate loneliness. You may feel that some social needs are unmet or that connections lack the depth you desire. This is common and can be improved with intentional effort toward building meaningful relationships.
suggestions: Actively seek opportunities to join social activities, interest groups, or community organizations where you can meet like-minded people
Focus on deepening existing friendships by initiating meaningful conversations and spending quality time together
Consider volunteering, as helping others often creates natural opportunities for social bonding and a sense of belonging
Practice reaching out to friends or family regularly, even when you do not feel like it, as social momentum builds over time
Reflect on whether any patterns of thinking or behavior may be contributing to feelings of disconnection

ranges.label: High Loneliness
ranges.description: Your score reflects high loneliness that may be affecting your emotional well-being, sleep, and mental health. Chronic loneliness at this level is associated with increased risk of depression and anxiety, and proactive intervention is important.
suggestions: Consider seeking professional counseling or therapy, as a therapist can help identify barriers to connection and develop social strategies
Explore structured social settings such as classes, support groups, or team activities where regular attendance fosters familiarity
Social skills training or assertiveness workshops can help if you find it difficult to initiate or maintain conversations
Limit excessive use of social media as a substitute for in-person interaction, as it can sometimes increase feelings of loneliness
Practice self-compassion and recognize that loneliness is a common human experience, not a personal failing

ranges.label: Severe Loneliness
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe loneliness likely having significant impact on both mental and physical health. Prolonged loneliness is linked to depression, cardiovascular issues, and weakened immunity. Professional help is strongly recommended.
suggestions: Seeking professional psychological help is strongly recommended, as severe loneliness often co-occurs with depression and anxiety that respond well to treatment
Cognitive-behavioral therapy can help challenge negative thought patterns about relationships and social interactions
If you are experiencing depressive symptoms alongside loneliness, please also address your emotional health with a clinician
Start with small, manageable social steps rather than overwhelming yourself with large social commitments
Crisis support is available if loneliness is causing thoughts of self-harm; contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline
